entry_description: AI agents execute a series of tasks to achieve a goal. Because agents and the underlying model cannot reliably distinguish instructions from related content, attackers can hijack the agent's goal to pursue adversarial objectives.
register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Agent goal hijack is the autonomous-agent hijacking risk (MR-071), achieved via prompt injection (MR-010); context poisoning (MR-071.2) is the finer sub-risk.

entry_description: Agents can misuse legitimate tools due to prompt injection, misalignment or unsafe/ambiguous delegation, leading to data exfiltration, tool-output manipulation or workflow hijacking.
register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Insecure integration with external tools, plugins and APIs
note: Tool misuse and exploitation maps to abuse of agent tool invocation (MR-071.1) and insecure tool/integration (MR-020), with data exfiltration through tools (MR-071.6).

entry_description: Identity and privilege abuse exploits dynamic trust and delegation in agents to escalate access and bypass controls by manipulating delegation chains, role inheritance, control flows and agent context (including cached credentials).
register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
note: Identity and privilege abuse maps to agent credential theft (MR-071.5) and abuse of valid accounts against the AI system (MR-015.1), within the excessive-agency risk (MR-071).

entry_description: Agentic supply chain vulnerabilities arise when agents, tools and related artefacts are provided by third parties and may be malicious, compromised or tampered with in transit (models, weights, tools, plugins).
register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
note: Agentic supply chain maps to poisoned AI agent tools published (MR-018.6) and poisoning of AI agent tools (MR-071.13) under the AI supply-chain risk (MR-018).

entry_description: Agentic systems often generate and execute code; attackers exploit code-generation features or embedded tool access to escalate into remote code execution (RCE), local misuse or exploitation of internal systems.
register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
note: Unexpected code execution via an agent maps to abuse of agent tool invocation (MR-071.1) and container or sandbox escape (MR-015.8); insecure code generation (MR-019) is related.

entry_description: Agentic systems rely on stored and retrievable context (memory, conversation history, retrieved data); poisoning that context corrupts continuity and reasoning across tasks.
register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Memory and context poisoning is the register's agent context poisoning (MR-071.2) and RAG poisoning (MR-010.6).

entry_description: Multi-agent systems depend on continuous communication between autonomous agents coordinating via APIs, message buses and shared memory, significantly expanding the attack surface and undermining perimeter-based security.
register_names: Multi-agent interaction risks | Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
note: Insecure inter-agent communication is the multi-agent interaction risk (MR-057); agent-to-agent abuse channels relate to command and control (MR-071.12).

entry_description: Agentic cascading failures occur when a single fault (hallucination, malicious input, corrupted tool or poisoned memory) propagates across autonomous agents, compounding into system-wide harm and bypassing stepwise human checks.
register_names: Multi-agent interaction risks | Vendor/model concentration, monoculture and correlated failure
note: Cascading failures across autonomous agents map to the multi-agent interaction risk (MR-057), with correlated-failure overlap at MR-047. OWASP ASI08 gives MR-057 a direct external anchor.

entry_description: Agents can establish strong trust with users through fluency, emotional intelligence and perceived expertise (anthropomorphism); adversaries or misaligned designs exploit this trust to influence decisions or extract sensitive information.
register_names: Manipulation, persuasion and dark patterns | Anthropomorphism, emotional dependence and psychological harm | Overreliance and automation bias
note: Human-agent trust exploitation maps to manipulation and dark patterns (MR-030), emotional dependence (MR-036) and overreliance (MR-034); deceptive content baiting agents is MR-071.9.

entry_description: Rogue agents are malicious or compromised AI agents that deviate from their intended function or authorized scope, acting harmfully, deceptively or parasitically within multi-agent or human-agent ecosystems.
register_names: Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
note: Rogue agents map to adversary-deployed AI agent in the environment (MR-071.11) and agent abused for command and control (MR-071.12) under the excessive-agency risk (MR-071).
